video_title: Tokenized Assets Hit $15 Billion (Here's What's Next) | Ian de Bode
transcript: Tokenized assets are exploding in value. Quite frankly, we've gone with tokenized treasuries as a category. Two years ago, that category as a whole stood at $1 billion. Right now, we're coming up on $15 billion15. And everyone's looking at Robin Hood. They want to be Robin Hood. What does Robin Hood mean? It means, you know, crypto, but also stocks, ETFs, prediction markets, all of these things. With tokenized stocks, they can use their existing custody infrastructure, their existing accounting systems. They can keep the market open 24/7 and you could do stable coin settlements on these things, right? You can't do that with a normal stock setup. You should prepare for a world in which tokenized securities coexist with normal securities. So, get ready. Ian, thanks for being here in at Consensus. We were just discussing to actually be doing this podcast in Dutch, but we will keep it by English. Um the first question that I wanted to ask is what is the current stage of tokenized assets? Where are we heading to? What are you actually seeing in the markets nowadays? Just what is the current stage of tokenized assets? >> Yeah, it's a great question because there's obviously been a lot happening. If you just look at the numbers, tokenized assets are exploding in value. Quite frankly, we've gone with tokenized treasuries as a category. 2 years ago that category as a whole stood at $1 billion. Right now we're coming up on $15 billion 15. >> Yeah. >> We've seen similar growth in other asset classes whether that's you private credit has had its moment uh but most recently from us tokenized stocks and ETFs that as an asset class was really just brought on chain last year in June as a starter and already we're starting to cross a billion and a half. So you're seeing explosive growth in all of these tokenized assets because all the work from an infrastructure perspective etc has more or less now been done right more and more assets are coming on chain the stable coins really the first instance of a tokenized assets that grew pretty explosively there's a lot of liquidity on chain there's a lot of capital on chain that capital is looking to invest in other assets and those assets are making their way on chain thanks to companies like so you're really just seeing explosive growth quite frankly and it's not slowing down. >> If you look at all the different exchanges where you can start trading those tokenized assets, >> where is the demand coming from for those products? >> Yeah, for for on those products in particular, all of that is outside of the US, right? So, our assets are actually listed on Binance, on OKX, on BitGate, Gate, Minamask, Phantom, you name it. like large crypto wallets and large crypto exchanges are now all listing tokenized stocks. All of these crypto distribution platforms have had crypto and cryptoreated products for a while, but they're all looking to be the everything app, right? Everyone's looking at Robin Hood. They want to be Robin Hood. What does Robin Hood mean? It means, you know, crypto, but also stocks, ETFs, prediction markets, all of these things. For a lot of those crypto distribution partners, it is much easier to support stocks in tokenized form than it is in call it normal form. Right? With tokenized stocks, they can use their existing custody infrastructure, their existing accounting systems. They can keep the market open 24/7 and you can do stable coin settlements on these things, right? You can't do that with a normal stock setup. So for a lot of these crypto distribution platforms, the Binances, the OKXs of the world, it is much easier to support these things in tokenized form. And so that's where we've really seen the demand is these distribution platforms listing these stocks and offshore investors finally having access to US capital markets in a very seamless way in the platform that they're used to trading anyway. So people just, you know, go between crypto and stocks very seamlessly, instantly over the weekend, no problem. But if we if we have so many interests coming in from outside of the US to buy those equities that are in the US, um there's always the security layer like if you buy a tokenized asset, then you're not owning the actual stock. >> Mhm. >> So how do you manage that type of risk? Yeah. >> So with R, there's multiple different ways to tokenize, right? And there's a lot of debate about what's the right way. You've got people that say, "No, no, the the only right way to tokenize if by tokenizing the native stock, right?" So then when you hold a token onchain, it's the actual stock itself. You've got other companies that have really tried to make a business out of that. But if you look at the numbers, no one's really buying those things. And I think that there's two big problems with that model. Although I to be clear, it should exist because it can have some benefits. But if you do it in native form, the problem with it is that you need the issuer consent. So you need the actual public company to say yes, I want to have my token, my shares in tokenized form as well. Most of the big companies that people want to buy, think of your Tesla and your Google and your whatever >> and well anthropic pretty soon, but like they don't really they don't care about tokenizing their stock, right? They're just like we'll list on the NASDAQ or the New York Stock Exchange. anyone can buy it there. Good enough for them, right? The the companies that care about native issuance have been either cryptonnative companies or quite frankly companies that are maybe struggling and looking for a new distribution avenues to find more buyers of their stock. But you're dealing a little bit with an adverse selection problem and there's not a lot of choice in that native category. Maybe there's like five companies total, right? On top of that, if you want to hold the thing in native form, you need to KYC with the transfer agent and you can only then register your wallet that you can hold that particular stock in. You can't transfer it out of your wallet seamlessly like you would a stable coin. You can't use the thing in DeFi. So really what you're doing is you're kind of reinventing a brokerage account, a normal investing account, but with less stocks and that's it, right? So we don't really see at this point in the current state what that model really gets you. The other way to tokenize is what we focused on which are permissionless rappers. Right? So you can take you can go offshore and you have an issuing company and there you can issue a new type of security. Ours are debt instruments against the issuer that you can hold and it represents full economic exposure to the underlying. So whenever on the finance or the on the global markets the issuer means call it tokenized Tesla Tesla on that token is a debt instrument. So you're holding a debt claim against the issuer security interest on the collateral. The issuer then buys Tesla on the open market keeps it in a custody account so that for every single token outstanding there's an actual Tesla stock backing that. What you hold on chain is a debt instrument. Meaning that you have a security interest in the collateral that is what we call perfected by a third party. There's a third party that is not the issuer that has access to our brokerage accounts that looks at it to make sure that we are fully backed and then sell. Right? It's a third independent party. They post daily at the stations that anyone can access that says this is how much the issuer holds in all the various stocks and they can you know pair that to the tokens we have outstanding. If anything ever happens to us as the issuer or if we fail to meet a redemption in a timely matter or god knows what, that third party has legal authority to claim all of the assets in the brokerage account, sell them for cash, repay all the debt holders, which are the token holders that are on chain. So, it's very it's kind of sophisticated investor protections. >> You do not hold the same thing as the stock onchain. That is absolutely correct. But the benefit of doing it that way is that you can issue these tokens in a permissionless wrapper way. Very much like a stable coin, right? A stable coin you can hold it in your wallet, but you can freely transfer it to any other wallet you want, right? You can also use it in DeFi on a wherever, however you want, 24/7. No one there's no permissions on it. >> Yeah. >> Our tokenized stocks are the same way. They're fully permissionless rappers. If you want to mint and burn them directly from us, you have to onboard with us. But in the secondary market, you can send them freely wherever. And you can use them in DeFi, however, but you only get that benefit because you've structured these things as permissionless rappers because it is not the native stock. >> And quite frankly, this is how tokenized stock tokenized assets more broadly have taken off. When you think about what is a stable coin, >> stable coin is not native cash. Like the the equivalent of native cash would probably be a tokenized bank deposit. No one's using tokenized bank deposits on chain in part because they would be fully KYC. You can't use it in DeFi. That's true. >> A stable coin is a permissionless wrapper. That is economic exposure to the dollar where it is backed by dollar sitting in a bank account or treasuries at a custody account. Our tokenized stocks, ETFs, and treasuries are exactly like that. They're just an extension of stable coins. And as a result, they work. They work at DeFi. They work for distribution. And we've seen real demand on them. >> How do you um I think the interesting part here is that if you have well actually works the same as what IBIT is doing with Bitcoin ETF. If someone purchases the IBIT uh ETF, you need to buy Bitcoin and that's why we see the price of Bitcoin move up and down. Actually, the past few days, it was a large inflow in the ETF, which is great for any Bitcoin holder. But, >> yep. >> Aside from that, um what do you see as the next few steps? I think you touched upon that for a little bit, but by having tokenized stocks, you can then also use that those stocks for collateral in DeFi protocols or to generate additional yield 100%. So that's where it gets really fun and interesting, right? Because for this thing to really take off, it's my firm belief that once your stock on chain is better than what you can have in a brokerage account, that's when this thing really scales. But for that to happen, I think a couple more things need to need to occur. First and foremost, we need to have more stocks and ETFs on chain, right? So that people can have a full portfolio. And then you need to be able to get margin on these stocks just like you would at a brokerage account. Although in a brokerage account, you're fully locked into the platform. Whatever the rate is that the brokerage account offers you, that's the rate you're getting. And oftent times, those rates are very, very high. And these companies know they can charge you that rate because what are you going to do? Are you going to transfer your stocks to a different broker? That process takes forever. All right. That's true. With tokenized stocks, you can freely transfer these things around between wallets. You can go shop around rates for margin in DeFi. We're already starting to see some vaults like we have a Boro vault uh on our assets that people can use for collateral purposes. But what's then also really exciting is how do you take these tokenized stocks and take them as collateral and move more towards almost like a prime brokerage experience where you can seamlessly get leverage on your positions and your collateral. maybe in the form of per right so has been uh developing the ono per platform uh as well we'll launch that pretty soon there the key innovation is going to be using your tokenized stocks as collateral for pers on those same names that's dramatically going to improve capital efficiency uh on perpetual swaps as an instrument and really give new utility to these tokenized stocks where people going to be able to use it as collateral to even do a carry trade It's also going to create more chain streets I guess if we uh with all the new assets on uh on on chain. There are so many market makers that are looking to actually trade the markets which I think is also good because it makes the markets more efficient. The question then is then if we are able to use tokenized stocks as collateral in DeFi protocols and basically define the loop again. >> Yeah. >> How do we prevent ourselves from going into a big bubble? I mean for the nice thing about the equities market is that it is so large. Yeah. Right. Uh when you look at bubbles it's either historically because you know the assets themselves became inflated and rapidly crashed and volatility was very high or people start playing looping games right that's always possible. Now if people start playing looping games on these assets in DeFi I mean >> in part that is the beauty of DeFi and that it is permissionless. So, I'm sure people will figure it out. And quite frankly, that's not that dissimilar to how spot margin works in Trafi, right? Like you can go like big institutional funds, hedge funds can go 10x long easily with some of these assets by essentially pledging their stocks as collateral, getting cash, buying more of a stock, pledging as collateral, getting cash, buying more. like all these s systems have been automated in tradi. But you know there's no reason why it wouldn't be able to work in DeFi. The benefit here though is that you have quality collateral right if you do a looping strategy on the S&P 500 that's more or less what a lot of funds are doing anyway right but now it's open to anyone globally with an internet connection stable coins and the actual tokenized stock. So I do think it is partly democratizing finance and that ultimately is what honor really is about is like creating a more open access financial ecosystem globally. >> The qu I think recently we've seen many DeFi protocols having exploits hacks which kind of shows the phase that we're in. How do you see DeFi proceed from here? There's a big debate happening whether we actually should be having DeFi, >> right, >> or a better version of the current financial system. >> Yes, I think it's a good debate to have. I mean, obviously the hacks have been pretty painful. Um, >> and it's also a discussion on how to like like A has been collectively getting assistance from all the protocols out there. Yeah. >> Which I think is a great governance system. >> Yep. But there's also the question of like should we be having this or should such a protocol just go down because it's it has this exploit and then better versions will come up again right you know in finance traditional finance there is a backs stop on all of this right it typically is a central bank but it's very common for there to be some sort of a backs stop because if if trust in a system is gone people start pulling liquidity and that just cascades leads down a system often with rather catastrophic consequences and you started seeing that a little bit in DeFi where it wasn't just a people were pulling money out of DeFi more broadly right that is that doesn't really help anyone so for there to be a backs stop I think is very helpful so it's better to have a backs stop than necessarily let some protocols as long as they're you good and systemically important to let them fail I think the difference here with DeFi and Trafi is when the Fed bills something out like they did in the global financial crisis is is the taxpayers that end up footing the bill >> whereas here in DeFi it's not true quite frankly a lot better um but it you know DeFi is not perfect I think that much is clear but it does come with meaningful benefits too right it is a fully permissionless system that can work 24/7 that is fully collateralized and that has been able to provide real services to a global population that oftentimes has not had access to these services those are really meaningful benefits those for like 0ero to one innovations with real human impact. Now, is the system perfect? No. Like every system has positives and downsides, but I do feel like at times there's been more almost like a little bit of grave dancing around DeFi. It's like, haha, I told you it's not good. Whereas people forget all the good qualities and benefits that it has. On top of that, I would also just highlight, you know, security. It's good that there's an emphasis on security again, right? with all these hacks, I think people >> reappreciate that when you think about security, it isn't just, you know, multi-IGs and is my smart contract secure. It's also how you've designed the protocol. What do you accept as collateral? Um because not all collateral is the same, right? And with that also again in mind, having tokenized stocks, tokenized ETFs, having quality collateral come into these systems, I'm sure will actually benefit them. There is a way to structure all of this that I do think is fundamentally safe or safer, but it is good that people are aware of the risks they're taking when they deposit their money into a smart contract and they should be rewarded for taking that risk. And often times what I've seen in DeFi is the rates that people are getting are not commensurate with the risk and then quite frankly they should not be putting it into a smart contract. They should just put it in tokenized treasuries instead. Before we continue with this episode, I would like to thank my sponsors. And one of them is OKX. OKX is the place where I'm trading futures and doing day trades. And the reason that I'm using OKX is because it's a final month before the Mica license is going to be in play. Which means that if your exchange doesn't have the Mica license, it can actually cancel everything of your trades and ban you completely in your country. And that's why it's super important to use an exchange that is Mica compliant. And that's why I moved everything into OKX as it has three licenses already. And they are running a deposit match which means that you get a bonus up to 5% on your deposit if you go to OKX. Make sure to use our link and start trading on OKX. Now, let's get back to the episode. And I think if you look at the exploits and if you have one validator then >> yeah it's common sense that it's not going to be good. >> So ultimately it's the the market dynamics that create better versions of DeFi through trial and error and then we get into a better phase. >> Yep. >> What we've also been seeing is that I think it's the name is Shamat. >> He has been launching a new fund >> that wants to invest into preipos. >> Yep. and then tokenize that and gets the opportunity for retail to invest into it. And that brings me to the question of okay so we have tokenized stocks. When we look at the demand for tokenized stocks or those markets it's quite a well there are few ones that are doing the volumes and the rest is just not interesting enough. >> Mhm. How I can imagine that if you list entropic preipo or any of those big ones, there's a huge demand for that market. >> I bet. Yeah, there's a lot of FOMO access to >> everybody is using cloud code nowadays. Anyways, so my question is like is that the next step in looking to list preipo projects or products or other versions that are getting so much demand in terms of um volume and interest. >> Yeah. >> That is then also being opened to more than just institutional investors. How do you see that? >> Yeah, it's a very good question. I get there's a lot of talk about it. Yeah, I I bet uh there's obviously a lot of FOMO about people wanting to get exposure to these like gamechanging companies, right? Like Anthropic, SpaceX, Open AI. And I think that's that's good. The the problem with these private markets and assets ultimately is those companies have a lot more control over their cap table and who holds the actual stock. There's also significantly more questions around what really is the value of this thing, right? Because the value of something is correlated with the liquidity that is available to buy it, right? If there is low liquidity available, which is the fact on these private market assets, it doesn't take that much money to push up the price. Now, does that mean that that is the actual price of the asset? Not necessarily. If this thing lists on the NASDAQ or the New York Stock Exchange with like 10 or 100x the volume and liquidity available, the price may crater, right? And I'm pretty sure no one wants that either. So, it's a tricky thing to pull off. The benefit of focusing just on public stocks and ETFs is that those companies the moment they list on, you know, a big exchange and they go public, they relinquish control over their cap table. They can't control who buys a stock or not, right? In the private markets, they most certainly often times can. So, there's been instances where people tried to tokenize the private market stuff. A a large company all of a sudden figured it out and said, "Wait, what are you doing? You're not supposed to do that." And basically shut it down, right? And what do you then do with those people who bought into an SPV that thought they held the asset, but they didn't really, right? So, it just comes with more investor risk both on the price and the actual holdings, which is why we haven't necessarily focused there. It doesn't mean that we never will, but it's quite frankly not been as high of a priority for us. There's just so much to do on the public stocks, ETFs, the perpetuals, etc. that that is historically where we focused. >> What are the products that you're most excited about coming up in the next year aside from like just the biggest stocks? >> Yeah, I think perpetual swaps are incredibly interesting >> just as a financial instrument, right? The I perp is the preferred method of trading for leverage in crypto. >> But perp on single name equities, even commodities, ETFs, it's not. >> People in traffic don't know what a purpose. They know futures, right? >> Yeah. They don't know what a purpose is. >> They have an expiration date. >> Yeah. Exactly. You tell that to someone in crypto and they're like, "Wait, an expiration date?" Why? So that as an instrument and starting to apply that to single name stocks, ETFs, commodities, industries, kind of what you're seeing with what Trade XYZ is doing on Hyperlquid now. And then combining that with stocks as collateral, I think will be quite the interesting innovation, significantly more capital efficient for hedging. It opens up the ability to do a carry trade on stocks. Right now, you can only do a carry trade on on crypto. That market is tapped out, right? if you can if you can crack the carry trade or like perpetuals on stocks, that thing will be significantly larger in terms of market size than crypto. So, I think that's going to be very interesting to see. >> Yeah, I'm a uh a uh volatility trader myself with the fund. So if I go into hyperlquid and I've got the ability to basically trade gold and bitcoin and the fees are so low, it's super for my strategy to continue trading through perks as of the capital efficiency on there but also by reducing the cost that if I talk to a regular options trader, the amount of fees they need to pay when there's an expiry is just ridiculous. >> Yeah, it's pretty absurd. Um then the issue is is that if I trade on for instance hyperlquid we are transitioning from 5 days a week to seven days a week but unfortunately um during the weekend there's not much of volume taking place on those assets. How how long do you think it's going to take until we definitely going to see 24/7 market? >> Yeah, you need liquidity over the weekend in order to get real traded, right? Unless there's like the true market dislocation event and people really want to trade the thing. That's what we saw with the Iran crisis and oil markets are hyperlquid, right? That was sufficiently large of a market dislocation that people figured out how to onboard to hyperlquid, put more liquidity into the books because it actually made sense and get real price signals off of it. for doing that for other markets particularly when there's no other venue to trade right for like single stocks and the like the NASDAQ the New York Stock Exchange they close over the weekend right you can't actually trade a thing there so it's significantly harder to do because also no one really knows what the price of the thing is like who's going to take the price risk who's going to actually create the book I will say the new the NASDAQ and New York Stock Exchange have been very public about moving towards 24/7 >> trade >> ideally even this year so I think once that happens, there's enough price signals on those large venues, even over the weekend. It's not going to be super deep liquidity, but at least some liquidity better than what exists now. Can those price signals inform the per and are people going to get more comfortable with deploying those markets over the weekend? I think the answer to that 100% is yes. So, once those big venues start moving towards 247 markets, I think PERPS will really go to 24/7 markets. Or maybe it's even the opposite, right? pers go faster on the 24/7 and that creates price signals in the spot market. So I think the interplay between these markets is going to be very interesting to watch. So kind of similar with Bitcoin. Sometimes the pers are moving, sometimes it's spot market because usually when it's spot driven it's stronger than when the pes are moving. But um interesting and if we look at policies and the clarity act and the genius act what can we expect in that sense? Um the Genius Act has been approved but it's not yet applicable. It's later I think in November of this year. >> Yeah. The rules and rigs are getting written. >> Yeah. Um and the Clarity Act is still not approved. Correct. >> What can we see from these two as an impact on tokenized assets and tokenization in general? >> Yeah. I think what Genius already did is signal that stable coins are here to stay. They're not going anywhere. And it's be it's it's causing a bit of a mad scramble among the banks in particular to try to figure out how do we deal with this stable coin thing, right? Because everyone knows it's not going wet. When you look at the volumes and the TVL into stable coins, it's only gone up since, right? More and more institutional capital is flowing in because now for the first time there is a framework at least for stable coins. Even though if the specific rules haven't necessarily all been finalized, the big institutions know stable coins are here to stay. So they're they're they're flowing into it. I think market structure will cause a similar thing for tokenized securities more broadly. Even though when you look at market structure, there's not that much in there for tokenized securities. It basically just says, hey, tokenized securities are great. Uh they're here to stay. The SEC is going to have full authority over rulemaking, right? Yeah. Um but then it's still going to take the SEC to come up with a bunch of rules around these tokenized securities. But that in and of itself, I think will have a similar impact that Genius had on stable coins. Basically signaling, hey, this stuff isn't going away. You should prepare for a world in which tokenized securities coexist with normal securities, so get ready. Interesting. Well, it uh it is shorter than a regular episode, but I'm happy that you've been on here to discuss something that maybe retail investors are not yet understanding completely, but it will come to them. they will be using these products because everything onchain is way better than in the current financial system that we have. That's also what we see here at consensus. >> Amen. >> So, uh thank you very much for being here. Um looking forward to be having this discussion again in a year or in two years when hopefully everything is tokenized. >> Very good. Looking forward to it. >> Thank you. Thanks.
